request.question: Londonderry Borough Police are the force in the county that is part of what country?

title: Reading Borough Police
Londonderry County Borough Council election, 1920
St Ives Borough Police
County Londonderry
Penzance Borough Police
Law enforcement in Ireland
Newcastle upon Tyne City Police
Londonderry Borough Police
Folkestone Borough Police
Ferguson baronets
sentences: Reading Borough Police was a police force for the borough of Reading in the United Kingdom.

The force was created on 21 February 1836, at which time it had a strength of 30 constables, two sergeants and two inspectors. Towards the end of the 19th century, Reading Borough Police had increased in size to 62 officers.
However, the local population had risen to around 60,500, which meant one officer for every 1,000 inhabitants.
By the time of the First World War the force had an establishment of 113 officers, however, due to military service only 30 officers were patrolling Reading.
An election to Londonderry County Borough Council took place in January 1920 as part of that year's Irish local elections.
The Local Government (Ireland) Act 1919 had seen elections for local government in Ireland change to a more proportional system.
As a result Unionists lost control over Londonderry borough council, which for the first time passed into the control of the city's Catholic majority.
St Ives Borough Police was the borough police force for the town of St Ives, Cornwall, from 1836 to 1889.
It was established following the passage of the Municipal Corporations Act 1835.
The force was unique in that it only boasted a single constable at any one time throughout its entire existence.
Since 1972, the counties in Northern Ireland; including Londonderry; have no longer been used by the state as part of the local administration.
Following further reforms in 2015, the area is now governed under three different districts; Derry and Strabane, Causeway Coast and Glens and Mid-Ulster.
Despite no longer being used for local government and administrative purposes, it is sometimes used in a cultural context in All-Ireland sporting and cultural events (ie - Derry GAA).
Penzance Borough Police was the police force for the borough and corporate town of Penzance, Cornwall, from 1836 to 1947.
It was formed following the passing of the Municipal Corporations Act 1835, which reformed all UK boroughs, and stipulated that each appoint a Watch Committee to oversee a police force.
The police force formed part of the commonality of the town’s government, led by an elected Mayor, six aldermen and eighteen councillors.
This is a description of law enforcement in Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland.

Before the Republic (then called the Irish Free State) left the union, one police force - the Royal Irish Constabulary - policed almost the whole island (aside from Dublin, where the Dublin Metropolitan Police were the main force, Belfast where the Belfast Borough Police were the main force and the borough of Londonderry where the Londonderry Borough Police were the main force before merging with the RIC.
See List of defunct law enforcement agencies in the United Kingdom and Irish Police records and also Royal Irish Constabulary Forum for other forces in Ireland that have been disbanded.)
Newcastle upon Tyne City Police also known as Newcastle upon Tyne Borough Police, was the Home office Police force and a Constabulary that served the City of Newcastle upon Tyne with origins in 1836.
On 1 April 1969 it was amalgamated into the Northumberland Constabulary under the Police Act 1964.
The first Modern Police Force was formed in Newcastle in 1836, 3 years before the County Police Act of 1839.
The Londonderry Borough Police was the police force in the city of Derry, County Londonderry, Ireland from 1848 to 1870, nicknamed the Horney Dicks after the bones used in their helmets.
They replaced the earlier town watch and were in turn replaced by the Royal Irish Constabulary.
The police force was established by the Londonderry Improvement Act 1848 and were governed by the Londonderry Corporation.
Its abolition was recommended by a commission of inquiry into sectarian riots in the city in 1869.

The commission felt the force, having the form of a town watch, was inadequate to policing serious crime; it also noted, but did not endorse, allegations that the police discriminated against Roman Catholics.
Folkestone Borough Police was the borough police force for the town of Folkestone in Kent.
It was formed in 1851 under the terms of the Municipal Corporations Act 1835.
The Ferguson Baronetcy, of the City of Londonderry, was a title in the Baronetage of the United Kingdom.
It was created on 7 October 1801 for Andrew Ferguson, who had previously represented Londonderry Borough in the Irish House of Commons.
The second Baronet represented Londonderry in the British House of Commons.
The title became extinct on his death in 1860.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: Londonderry Borough Police are the force in the county that is part of what country?
hop1_docs: Londonderry Borough Police | The Londonderry Borough Police was the police force in the city of Derry, County Londonderry, Ireland from 1848 to 1870, nicknamed the Horney Dicks after the bones used in their helmets.  They replaced the earlier town watch and were in turn replaced by the Royal Irish Constabulary.  The police force was established by the Londonderry Improvement Act 1848 and were governed by the Londonderry Corporation.  Its abolition was recommended by a commission of inquiry into sectarian riots in the city in 1869.  The commission felt the force, having the form of a town watch, was inadequate to policing serious crime; it also noted, but did not endorse, allegations that the police discriminated against Roman Catholics.

Law enforcement in Ireland | This is a description of law enforcement in Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland.  Before the Republic (then called the Irish Free State) left the union, one police force - the Royal Irish Constabulary - policed almost the whole island (aside from Dublin, where the Dublin Metropolitan Police were the main force, Belfast where the Belfast Borough Police were the main force and the borough of Londonderry where the Londonderry Borough Police were the main force before merging with the RIC.  See List of defunct law enforcement agencies in the United Kingdom and Irish Police records and also Royal Irish Constabulary Forum for other forces in Ireland that have been disbanded.) .

Londonderry County Borough Council election, 1920 | An election to Londonderry County Borough Council took place in January 1920 as part of that year's Irish local elections.  The Local Government (Ireland) Act 1919 had seen elections for local government in Ireland change to a more proportional system.  As a result Unionists lost control over Londonderry borough council, which for the first time passed into the control of the city's Catholic majority.

Barnsley Borough Police | Barnsley Borough Police was the police force operating in county borough of Barnsley, England until 1 October 1968.  The force was merged with other forces in 1968 to form the West Yorkshire Constabulary.  The area passed to the South Yorkshire Police in 1974.

Penzance Borough Police | Penzance Borough Police was the police force for the borough and corporate town of Penzance, Cornwall, from 1836 to 1947.  It was formed following the passing of the Municipal Corporations Act 1835, which reformed all UK boroughs, and stipulated that each appoint a Watch Committee to oversee a police force.  The police force formed part of the commonality of the town’s government, led by an elected Mayor, six aldermen and eighteen councillors.

Doncaster Borough Police | Doncaster Borough Police was the police force operating in the county borough of Doncaster, England until 1 October, 1968.  The force was then merged with others to form a West Yorkshire Constabulary.  From 1974 its area passed to the South Yorkshire Police.
Abingdon Borough Police | Abingdon Borough Police was the police force responsible for policing the Borough of Abingdon, situated in the county of Berkshire, England until 1889.
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - Londonderry Borough Police — passage [1]: "city of Derry, County Londonderry, Ireland" (exact phrase).
  - Londonderry Borough Police — passage [2]: placed in context of "law enforcement in Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland" and notes borough of Londonderry before merging with RIC.
  - No passage explicitly states "County Londonderry is part of Northern Ireland" or "is part of the United Kingdom."

- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - Passage [1] uses "Ireland" (historical context, 1848–1870), which could mean the island of Ireland rather than the modern Republic.
  - Passage [2] references both Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland but does not explicitly assign County Londonderry to either modern polity.
  - Missing an explicit, unambiguous statement of the county's current country (Northern Ireland / United Kingdom vs Republic of Ireland).

- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - Need an explicit statement that identifies which country currently contains County Londonderry (e.g., "County Londonderry is in Northern Ireland" or "County Londonderry is part of the United Kingdom").
  - Or a contemporary authoritative source listing counties and their countries that includes County Londonderry.

- retrieval_direction (priority):
  1. County Londonderry — Wikipedia page (or authoritative county profile) — query: "County Londonderry country"
  2. Derry / Londonderry (city) — Wikipedia page — query: "Derry country" or "Derry Northern Ireland"
  3. List of counties of Northern Ireland / administrative divisions — query: "Counties of Northern Ireland list" or "Is County Londonderry in Northern Ireland?"
hop2_docs: Lord Lieutenant of the City of Londonderry | This is a list of people to have served as Lord Lieutenant of the city of Londonderry (originally formally "County of the City of Londonderry", later the "County Borough of Londonderry").
Londonderry, Guernsey County, Ohio | Londonderry is an unincorporated community in Londonderry Township, Guernsey County, Ohio, United States.
Custos Rotulorum of County Londonderry and Down | The Custos Rotulorum of Londonderry and Down was the highest civil officer in counties Londonderry and Down.
Marquess of Londonderry | Marquess of Londonderry, of the County of Londonderry ( ), is a title in the Peerage of Ireland.

McFarland baronets | The McFarland Baronetcy, of Aberfoyle in the County of Londonderry, is a title in the Baronetage of the United Kingdom.  It was created on 23 January 1914 for the Irish businessman John McFarland.  He was founder of and a partner in the firm of McCrea and McFarland, engineering contractors, of Belfast and Derry, Chairman of Mulhollands, drapers, and owner of the Lough Swilly Steamship Company, and also served as Mayor of Londonderry.  He was succeeded by his son, the second Baronet.  He was a businessman, a Senator of Northern Ireland, Mayor of Londonderry, Lord Lieutenant of the City of Londonderry and an Irish Rugby union international.  The third Baronet has been a member of the Londonderry Corporation and a Deputy Lieutenant of the City of the County of Londonderry from 1956 to 1982.

Roe Valley Country Park | The Roe Valley Country Park is a forested area containing part of the River Roe, south west of Limavady, County Londonderry, Northern Ireland.  It is maintained by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency, which is part of the Department of the Environment of Northern Ireland.
Londonderry Graving Dock railway station | Londonderry Graving Dock railway station served Derry in County Londonderry in Northern Ireland.
hop2_titles: Lord Lieutenant of the City of Londonderry
Custos Rotulorum of County Londonderry and Down
response.summary_2: Northern Ireland (part of the United Kingdom). Passages identify County Londonderry as being in Northern Ireland (e.g., Roe Valley Country Park and Londonderry Graving Dock refer to "County Londonderry, Northern Ireland").
response.answer: Northern Ireland (part of the United Kingdom)
